content_en:
Tabaqat ash-Shafi'iyyah al-Kubra by as-Subki is not theologically neutral. As-Subki was a committed Ash'ari theologian and a defender of the kalam tradition, and these commitments shaped how he wrote about Shafi'i scholars and about the broader theological history of the school.

As-Subki was particularly concerned with defending the Ash'ari theological tradition against what he regarded as the innovations of Ibn Taymiyyah and the Hanbali Athari school. His Tabaqat reflects this concern: he includes extended theological discussions in the biographies of scholars who were involved in theological controversies, defends Ash'ari positions on the divine attributes, and is critical of scholars he regarded as deviating from the Ash'ari consensus.

This theological orientation means that Tabaqat ash-Shafi'iyyah al-Kubra must be read with awareness of the author's perspective. As-Subki's characterizations of scholars who held Athari or Hanbali positions are sometimes unfair or polemical. Conversely, his treatment of Ash'ari scholars is often laudatory in ways that go beyond what the evidence strictly supports. Scholars consulting the work as a historical source need to distinguish between documented facts and as-Subki's interpretive framework.

At the same time, as-Subki's theological concerns make the work particularly valuable for understanding the relationship between the Shafi'i legal school and the Ash'ari theological school — a relationship that was not accidental. The Shafi'i school had historically been closely associated with the Ash'ari tradition: ash-Shafi'i's student al-Muzani was part of the intellectual environment from which al-Ash'ari emerged, and major Shafi'i scholars of subsequent generations — al-Juwayni, al-Ghazali, ar-Razi — were also prominent Ash'ari theologians. As-Subki's documentation of this connection, however colored by his own commitments, is historically valuable.

The extended theological discussions that as-Subki includes in the Tabaqat — discussions of specific theological controversies, defenses of Ash'ari positions, and analyses of where individual scholars stood on disputed questions — make the work unusually rich as a source for the history of Islamic theology as well as for Islamic legal biography.
